candidates: Templar.set_temporary_context and copy_with_new_env pass None overrides through instead of ignoring them, causing variable_start_string=None to reach code that rejects None with TypeError.
Templar.set_temporary_context and copy_with_new_env apply override values without filtering out None, so variable_start_string=None is passed through instead of being ignored.
Templar.set_temporary_context and copy_with_new_env pass None override values through instead of ignoring them, causing the observed TypeError when variable_start_string=None.

Templar.set_temporary_context and copy_with_new_env pass None override values such as variable_start_string=None into the templating environment instead of filtering them out before applying overrides.

The bug is caused by Templar.set_temporary_context and copy_with_new_env not ignoring None overrides, _AnsibleMapping/_AnsibleUnicode/_AnsibleSequence not matching base type construction, and lookup/deprecation/CLI error paths not consistently formatting the active exception and help text.

The reported regressions are caused by several call sites assuming newer strict behavior from helper types/functions without preserving older permissive handling for None overrides, legacy YAML constructor signatures, and message formatting paths.
check_required_arguments only adds k to missing when k not in parameters, so a required parameter present with parameters[k] is None is incorrectly treated as provided.
check_required_arguments iterates with `k not in parameters` without handling `parameters is None`, causing a raw TypeError instead of a clear missing-arguments error for unset parameters.

check_required_arguments treats a required parameter whose value is None as present because it only checks k not in parameters, so unset required values bypass missing-argument handling and later produce unclear TypeError backtracebacks.

check_required_arguments assumes parameters is iterable and evaluates `k not in parameters`, so when parameters is None it raises an unhandled "'NoneType' is not iterable" TypeError instead of treating unset values consistently.

check_required_arguments and check_required_together assume parameters is iterable and do not guard parameters is None, so unset values trigger raw TypeError instead of clear missing-parameter handling.

check_required_arguments treats parameters containing required keys with None values as present while check_required_together and _validate_argument_types treat None as unset, causing inconsistent unset handling when _set_defaults inserts None into parameters.

The unclear active-exception traceback behavior is caused by `AnsibleModule.fail_json` treating an omitted `exception` argument as a request for only a raw traceback string, instead of reusing the active exception object when one exists.

The Ansible iptables module lacks match_set and match_set_flags in its parameter handling, so it never emits -m set --match-set in generated iptables commands.

The Ansible `iptables` module lacks `match_set` and `match_set_flags` handling, so it cannot translate those parameters into `-m set --match-set <setname> <flags>` in the generated iptables command.
The Ansible iptables module lacks match_set and match_set_flags parameters, so its command construction never emits -m set --match-set <setname> <flags>.

The Ansible `iptables` module’s parameter handling omits `match_set` and `match_set_flags`, so rule construction never emits `-m set --match-set <setname> <flags>`.
The bug is caused by the `iptables` module’s argument specification and command builder omitting concrete parameters for the iptables set match extension (`match_set` and `match_set_flags`).
UIStore.instance.windowWidth is not part of UIStore's UI state resize update path, so resize events do not update a readable UI state value for components.
UIStore.instance.windowWidth is being updated on resize but is not part of the UI state notifications that components subscribe to, so components do not react when it changes.
UIStore does not expose windowWidth as UI state that updates when a resize event changes UIStore.instance.windowWidth.

UIStore sets windowWidth from window.innerWidth in its constructor but only uses resizeObserverCallback for tracked Element dimensions, so window resize events never update UIStore.instance.windowWidth or emit UI_EVENTS.Resize for components.

UIStore updates windowWidth only via ResizeObserverCallback from ResizeObserver and never registers a window resize listener, so emitting a window resize event cannot update windowWidth or notify UI_EVENTS.Resize listeners.

UIStore initializes windowWidth from window.innerWidth but relies on ResizeObserver rather than a window resize listener, so manually emitted resize events do not update windowWidth or notify UI_EVENTS.Resize consumers.
UIStore's `windowWidth` is stale after viewport resize because the store never listens to the browser `window.resize` event and only relies on `ResizeObserver` entries for `document.body`.
UIStore.windowWidth stays stale because UIStore only updates it in resizeObserverCallback from document.body ResizeObserver entries and never listens to the window resize event.

UIStore.windowWidth is stale because resizeObserverCallback updates it from document.body contentRect rather than window.innerWidth, so a window resize that does not change document.body contentRect leaves UI_EVENTS.Resize consumers with the old width.

UIStore updates windowWidth only inside resizeObserverCallback from document.body contentRect via ResizeObserver, so window resize events that do not change document.body dimensions never update UIStore.instance.windowWidth or emit UI_EVENTS.Resize.
UIStore.windowWidth stays stale because UIStore only updates it in resizeObserverCallback from document.body ResizeObserver entries and never listens to the window "resize" event.

UIStore.windowWidth stays stale because UIStore only updates it in resizeObserverCallback from document.body ResizeObserver entries, so a window resize that changes window.innerWidth without changing document.body contentRect does not update UIStore.windowWidth or emit UI_EVENTS.Resize.

UIStore only updates windowWidth in resizeObserverCallback when ResizeObserver reports document.body, so a window resize that does not change document.body contentRect never updates UIStore.instance.windowWidth or emits UI_EVENTS.Resize.

Components cannot react to `UIStore.windowWidth` because the codebase has reactive hooks/selectors for other UI state values but no hook/accessor that subscribes to `UI_EVENTS.Resize` and returns `UIStore.instance.windowWidth`.
The voice recording system always uses fixed voice-optimized audio settings and does not check whether noise suppression is disabled before choosing encoding quality.

The voice recording system ignores the user's noise suppression setting and always applies fixed voice-optimized encoding settings.

The recorder uses a fixed low/voice-optimized encoder configuration and does not consult the user's noise suppression setting when constructing recording options.

parse appends a new cveContents object for each Trivy vulnerability occurrence and each Debian cvss3Severity without merging existing entries by type and cveID, so CVE-2013-1629 from python-pip and python-virtualenv produces duplicate trivy:ghsa/trivy:nvd records and split trivy:debian severities.

The bug is caused by server configuration code treating the `host` value as an opaque string and never passing it through an IP-network expansion/exclusion layer.
tomlloader.go loads each ServerInfo into Config.Servers without calling expandServerTargets, so CIDR host values remain literal and Ignore is never applied.
maskedPrefix in expandServerTargets changes a host-based CIDR like 192.168.1.1/30 into 192.168.1.0/30, so enumerateCIDR produces different targets than the configured Host implies.

tomlloader.go populates Config.Servers directly from ServerInfo.Host without using expandServerTargets or ServerInfo.Ignore, so CIDR hosts remain literal and SelectServers has no BaseName-expanded entries to select.

ServerInfo.Host is treated as a literal in tomlloader.go because configuration loading does not call expandServerTargets and the original ServerInfo lacked Ignore/BaseName data for exclusions and base-name selection.

config/tomlloader.go does not invoke expandServerTargets while loading c.Servers, so ServerInfo.Host CIDR values remain literal and Ignore/BaseName are never applied before subcmds use Config.SelectServers.

tomlloader populated Config.Servers directly from ServerInfo.Host without calling expandServerTargets, while subcmds selected targets by exact keys in c.Servers instead of using BaseName-aware SelectServers.

The remaining failure is caused by the expansion enumerating IPv4 network and broadcast addresses and/or using generated names that do not match the expected stable `base-<index>` naming for derived targets.
The Vuls2 database connection logic does not compare metadata.SchemaVersion against db.SchemaVersion before honoring SkipUpdate or deciding whether to download a new database.
The Vuls2 database connection logic checks SkipUpdate before comparing metadata.SchemaVersion with db.SchemaVersion, causing schema mismatches to bypass download/error handling.

The Vuls2 database connection logic checks only for metadata presence or update flags and never compares metadata.SchemaVersion to db.SchemaVersion, so mismatched existing databases are treated as valid and downloads are skipped.
The evidence does not yet single out the cause; inspect the Vuls2 database connection logic paths that read metadata.SchemaVersion, compare it with db.SchemaVersion, and apply SkipUpdate.
The Vuls2 database connection logic checks for existing metadata and SkipUpdate without comparing metadata.SchemaVersion against db.SchemaVersion, so mismatched schemas are treated as usable.
The Vuls2 database connection logic does not compare metadata.SchemaVersion against db.SchemaVersion before deciding whether to download or honor SkipUpdate.
The Vuls2 connection path checks for metadata presence and update timestamps but never compares `metadata.SchemaVersion` with `db.SchemaVersion`, so mismatched databases are treated as usable.
The caching policy treats pre-v7 remote clusters as if they expose RFD-28 resources, so it watches cluster_networking_config and cluster_audit_config instead of relying on legacy ClusterConfig.

The access point does not normalize legacy ClusterConfig from pre-v7 remotes into cluster_networking_config and cluster_audit_config, so the cache treats those RFD-28 resources as missing and repeatedly watches them.

The caching policy treats pre-v7 proxies as if they expose RFD-28 resources and watches cluster_networking_config and cluster_audit_config instead of legacy ClusterConfig, causing RBAC denials and cache re-inits.

The caching policy treats pre-v7 proxies like v7 remotes and watches cluster_networking_config and cluster_audit_config instead of legacy ClusterConfig, while the access point does not normalize ClusterConfig into the split resources.
The cache treats pre-v7 remote clusters as if they support RFD-28 split resources, so it watches cluster_networking_config and cluster_audit_config instead of using legacy ClusterConfig.

The cache resource selection for pre-v7 remote clusters includes `cluster_networking_config` and `cluster_audit_config` instead of legacy `ClusterConfig`, while the access point fails to normalize `ClusterConfig` into those split resources.
The cache resource selection logic treats pre-v7 remote clusters as if they support the split RFD-28 cluster configuration resources, causing watches for resources they cannot serve or authorize.

NewCachingAccessPointOldProxy uses a cache path that calls ClearLegacyFields before clusterConfigCache.SetClusterConfig while GetClusterAuditConfig and GetClusterNetworkingConfig still read split resources, so legacy ClusterConfig data from pre-v7 remotes is discarded instead of served.

clusterConfigCollection unconditionally calls ClearLegacyFields on ClusterConfig even for NewCachingAccessPointOldProxy, so a pre-v7 remote cache that relies on legacy ClusterConfig loses the data needed by GetClusterAuditConfig and GetClusterNetworkingConfig.

clusterConfigCollection.fetch clears legacy fields with ClusterConfig.ClearLegacyFields even for NewCachingAccessPointOldProxy caches that do not watch ClusterAuditConfig or ClusterNetworkingConfig, leaving rg.clusterConfig unable to serve GetClusterAuditConfig or GetClusterNetworkingConfig from legacy ClusterConfig data.

clusterConfig.ClearLegacyFields is called unconditionally when caching ClusterConfig, so NewCachingAccessPointOldProxy caches a pre-v7 ClusterConfig with legacy fields removed while not caching ClusterAuditConfig or ClusterNetworkingConfig.

clusterConfigCollection.fetch unconditionally calls clusterConfig.ClearLegacyFields before SetClusterConfig, so NewCachingAccessPointOldProxy caches a pre-v7 ClusterConfig with its legacy audit/networking fields removed while no split resources are available.

For NewCachingAccessPointOldProxy, the cache policy still depends on legacy ClusterConfig, but clusterConfigCollection.fetch clears legacy fields with ClearLegacyFields before clusterConfigCache.SetClusterConfig, so GetClusterAuditConfig and GetClusterNetworkingConfig have neither watched split resources nor usable legacy data.

The root cause is that the reverse tunnel cache selects the v7 remote proxy watch set for 6.x remotes and the ClusterConfig collection discards legacy embedded audit/networking data even when no split-resource collection is present to replace it.

Linked alternate-script fields are missed because `$6` linkage resolution exists only as a narrow `MarcBinary.get_linkage` method, while `MarcXml` has no equivalent and higher-level readers only consult a single matching 880 field.

parse.py relies on single-field get_linkage lookups from original fields, and MarcBinary.get_linkage only returns the first matching 880, so records whose alternate-script data is in multiple or one-way $6-linked 880 fields are omitted.

parse.py only uses MarcBinary.get_linkage for a single reciprocal 245 or 100 $6 link, while read_other_titles, read_authors, and read_contributions ignore linked 880 fields, causing alternate-script titles and names to be omitted.
